CSCE 442 Syllabus

Section 500 (46558)

Scientific Programming

Spring 2026 - College Station

Course Information

Meeting Times:  Meeting Type: LEC

Meeting Days: TR

Start Time: 09:35AM

End Time: 10:50AM

Start Date: 01/12/2026

End Date: 05/05/2026

Meeting Location:  HRBB 113

Credit Hours:  3

Instructor Details

Timothy Davis

Email:  davis@tamu.edu

Office Location:  235 Peterson

Phone:  979 845 4094

Office Hours

10am-11am, Tuesdays and Thursdays

Preferred Contact Method

email, text, or cell phone (cell: 352 359 2812)

Biography

Tim Davis is a Professor of Computer Science at Texas A&M University. His primary

scholarly contribution is the creation of widely-used sparse matrix algorithms and

software (SuiteSparse), including most of the sparse solvers behind x=A\b in

MATLAB, and many other commercial packages. For example, his codes power the

semiconductor processing of all high-end semiconductor fabs.  Every Apple device

uses his solvers, and they are also an integral part of Pokemen Go (for augmented

reality).  Fellow IEEE, SIAM, ACM.

Catalog Description

Scientific Programming. (3-0). Credit 3. Introduction to numerical algorithms

fundamental to scientific and engineering applications of computers; elementary

discussion of error; algorithms, efficiency; polynomial approximations, quadrature

and systems of algebraic and differential equations. Prerequisites: CSCE 221 with a

grade of C or better; MATH 304 or MATH 308 or concurrent enrollment.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  CSCE 221 with a grade of C or better; MATH 304 or

MATH 308 or concurrent enrollment.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

1. Apply computational arithmetic in real-world scenarios. Understand floating-

point numbers, rounding errors, relative and absolute errors, cancellation,

and numerical instability.

2. Solve linear and nonlinear equation systems, using Gaussian elimination, LU,

QR decompositions. Understand pivoting, norms, condition numbers, and

apply iterative methods, the bisection method, Newton’s method, the secant

1. Apply computational arithmetic in real-world scenarios. Understand floating-

point numbers, rounding errors, relative and absolute errors, cancellation,

and numerical instability.

2. Solve linear and nonlinear equation systems, using Gaussian elimination, LU,

QR decompositions. Understand pivoting, norms, condition numbers, and

apply iterative methods, the bisection method, Newton’s method, the secant

method.

3. Create and apply optimization techniques, based on the Golden section

search, the steepest descent method, and the Newton method, to solving

practical problems.

4. Perform data interpolation for approximation through the application of

Lagrange interpolation, Newton interpolation, Hermite interpolation, and

piecewise polynomial interpolation.

5. Apply numerical integration and differentiation, Richardson extrapolation, the

composite rule, Newton-Cotes quadrature, and Gaussian quadrature.

Special Course Designation

None

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Required

Scientific Computing: An Introductory Survey, Revised Second Edition

ISBN:  978-1-61197-557-4

Authors:  M. T. Heath

Publisher:  SIAM

Publication Date:  2018

Edition:  Revised 2nd Edition

URL for Resource:  https://doi.org/10.1137/1.9781611975581

Notes:

20% DISCOUNT AVAILABLE:  Go to:  https://doi.org/10.1137/1.9781611975581

Select NonMember Price(not Member Price).

At checkout enter coupon code: BKRSAD.

30% DISCOUNT for SIAM members:   If you are a SIAM student member, you can

get the Member Price discount instead (which is 30%).  Becoming a student

member of SIAM is free.

You can't use both discounts.

This material Is: Required

MATLAB

URL for Resource:  https://www.mathworks.com/academia/tah-portal/texas-am-

university-college-station-30976309.html

Notes:

You are required to have access to MATLAB. Texas A&M has a University-wide site

license, so as an A&M student, you can download it for free.

This material Is: Optional

Numerical Computing with MATLAB

URL for Resource:  https://www.mathworks.com/moler/index_ncm.html

Notes:

This book is optional (and free for download).  We will use some of the M-files from

this book, which I will provide for any given homework assignment.

Grading Policy

Exams 60%: we will have one in-class exams during the semester, and a final exam.

Each is worth 30% each.

Homework: 40%. Homework is always due at EXACTLY 9:35 AM on a class day, so

that I can discuss the solution in class that day. So normally I cannot accept late

this book, which I will provide for any given homework assignment.

Grading Policy

Exams 60%: we will have one in-class exams during the semester, and a final exam.

Each is worth 30% each.

Homework: 40%. Homework is always due at EXACTLY 9:35 AM on a class day, so

that I can discuss the solution in class that day. So normally I cannot accept late

submissions, except with prior permission. Homework is assigned, submitted, and

graded on Canvas. Unless otherwise noted on the assignment, all homework is to

be done by yourself. You may discuss the homework with each other but you must

solve the problems yourself. When discussing with each other, pretend you are in

my office discussing it with me. I will help you understand the problem but I

wouldn't give you the solution. If you do discuss the homework with anyone, state

their name on your submission (either on the HW itself or on a note on Canvas).

You may not use AI in any way.

Grading scale (after curving of individual items, as needed):

90 or higher: A

80: B

70: C

60: D

less than 60: F

The University views class attendance as the responsibility of an individual student.

Attendance is essential to complete the course successfully. University rules related

to excused and unexcused absences are located on-line at  http://student-

rules.tamu.edu/rule07 ; In general, I do not give excused absences for interviews or

travel, but exceptions are granted on a case-by-case basis. You must ask me

beforehand, however. If you miss class because of medical reasons or for an

interview (for work or for graduate / professional school) that cannot be

rescheduled, I reserve the right to require written confirmation. For interviews or

other scheduled events, a priori confirmation is required.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

No late assignments accepted without a priori permission.

Topics:

We will cover Chapters 1 to 8 of the textbook, but not all sections of those chapters.

Details are listed below, but these are subject to change:

1. Scientific computing:  sections 1 to 3, except skip 1.3.7 and 1.3.11 and

following

2. Linear systems: sections 1 to 5, except skip 2.4.8, 2.4.9, 2.5.2, and 2.5.3.  Also

skip the LDLT factorization (page 85)

3. Least squares: sections 1 and 2 (all).  Section 3: just the condition number.

Section 4: all.  Section 5: only 3.5.1.  Section 3.6: up to and including the

pseudo-inverse (page 139).  Section 7: all.

4. Eigenvalues:  Section 1: all except for the spectrum.  Section 2: just 4.2.1,

4.2.2, and 4.2.4 (but ignore the complex case).  Section 5: just 4.5.1 and 4.5.2.

5. Non-linear equations: Section 1: all.  Section 2: just the intermediate value

theorem for scalar functions.  Section 4: all.  Section 5: just subsections 1 to 4.

Section 5.6: just 5.6.2 (but skip quasi Newton methods)

6. Optimization:  Section 1: all.  Section 4: all (except skip 6.4.4).  Section 6.2.2.

Section 5: just 6.5.2 and 6.5.3.  We will skip Section 6, but as a side note,

Google solves this problem with their Ceres package, which relies on my

sparse solvers, to place every photo in StreetView.

7. Interpolation:  Section 1: all.  Section 3: just 7.3.1 and 7.3.2.  Section 4: just

7.4.1 and 7.4.2.

8. Integration: Section 1: all except skip Reimann sum and Riemann integrable

terms.  Section 3: only subsections 1, 5, and 6.

9. ODEs: we will cover this topic if time permits

AI Statement

No use of AI is permitted in any manner.

Course Specific Attendance Policy

Attendance is expected but not logged.
